children.name: Sphenodon
children.description: The tuatara. Sole surviving member of order Rhynchocephalia, whose lineage diverged from lizards 240 million years ago. It lived alongside dinosaurs and outlasted them. Despite superficial similarity to lizards, it is a deeply distinct lineage with a unique tooth-row shearing mechanism, a fully photoreceptive third eye on the top of the head, and the slowest-evolving DNA of any known vertebrate. It grows until age 35 and commonly lives over 100 years.

children.name: Sphenodon punctatus
children.description: Sole survivor of order Rhynchocephalia; lineage unchanged since the Triassic; has a fully photoreceptive third eye; grows until age 35; documented individuals over 110 years old; New Zealand's national reptile; restricted to predator-free offshore islands after mainland extinction caused by introduced rats and cats.

children.name: Sphenodon guntheri
children.description: A critically small population of tuatara restricted to North Brother Island in Cook Strait, New Zealand, recognised as a distinct species based on morphological and genetic differences from the Northern Tuatara. Adults tend to be smaller and paler than S. punctatus, with a more restricted diet dominated by wētā and other invertebrates. Conservation translocations to predator-free island sanctuaries have been essential to the population's survival.
